symbols: cheek-to-cheek embrace — the intimacy between mother and divine child, love incarnate
radiant halo on the child — divine glory of the incarnate God
Mary's red mantle — love and the blood of the Passion
Latin inscription at bottom — Song of Solomon text connecting the image to bridal mysticism
description: A devotional painting of a woman in a red mantle and white head covering holding a nude infant against her cheek. The child has a radiant golden halo and reaches up to touch her face. Their faces are pressed close together in a tender, cheek-to-cheek embrace. The background is dark blue-black. A Latin inscription runs along the bottom of the panel. The style is early Flemish with careful attention to skin tones and drapery. The overall mood is deeply intimate and devotional.
theological_description: Van der Weyden's Virgin and Child presents the Incarnation in its most tender, intimate form — the cheek-to-cheek embrace between Mary and the Christ child. The Latin inscription at the bottom references the Song of Solomon: 'His left arm is under my head, and his right arm embraces me' (Song of Solomon 2:6/8:3), connecting the maternal embrace to the bridal mysticism of the divine-human love relationship. The child's radiant halo declares his divine nature even as his human gestures — reaching for his mother's face — affirm the reality of the Incarnation: 'The Word became flesh and dwelt among us' (John 1:14). This is the God who 'made himself nothing by taking the very nature of a servant, being made in human likeness' (Philippians 2:7) — so fully human that he seeks his mother's warmth. Mary's tender response embodies her role as Theotokos and the model of all who receive Christ: 'Blessed is she who has believed that the Lord would fulfill his promises to her!' (Luke 1:45). The dark background isolates the sacred pair in a timeless, devotional space, inviting the viewer into contemplation of the mystery: God as an infant, the Creator cradled by a creature, the Almighty made vulnerable.

scripture_refs.reason: The Word made flesh — the incarnational mystery of God as a human child
The Song of Solomon text inscribed on the panel — the bridal embrace applied to Mary and Christ
Christ taking the form of a servant, made in human likeness — genuine human dependency
Blessed is she who believed — Mary's faith embodied in her embrace of the divine child
